column1: https://doi.org/10.15454/8ASYPC‡https://doi.org/10.15454/8ASYPC‡https://doi.org/10.15454/8ASYPC‡inrae://10.15454/8ASYPC‡‡doi:10.15454/8ASYPC‡2021-10-24T20:27:08Z‡2021-09-14T11:46:26Z‡‡Data for: Decomposition and N2O emissions of nine crop residues incubated under controlled conditions in two soils‡Lashermes
column3:
Gwenaëlle‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset was acquired to investigate the decomposition factors related to nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions during crop residues in soils under controlled conditions. Residues of sugar beet
column4: wheat
column5: rape seed
column6: potato
column7: pea
column8: mustard
column10: alfalfa
column12: varying by maturity at the time of collection
column13: were incubated in two soils during 60 days
column15: with a water-filled pore space of 60%. The residues contents in total carbon (C) and nitrogen (N)
column20: and lignin (LIG) were analyzed. The N2O fluxes
column22:
the dynamic of the soil contents in ammonium (NH4+) and nitrate (NO3-) were measured. Two layers of soil were prepared: a first one of 4 cm in the bottom without addition of residues and a second one of 4 cm in the surface in which the residues (manually cut into 1 cm pieces) were homogeneously mixed at a rate of 4 Mg dry matter (DM) ha-1. This work was supported by the ERA-NET FACCE ERA-GAS in the frame of the “Improved Estimation and Mitigation of Nitrous Oxide Emissions and Soil Carbon Storage from Cop Residues (Residue Gas)” project. FACCE ERA-GAS has received funding from the European Union’s Horizon 2020 Research and Innovation Program under grant agreement No. 696356. The French funding grant number was ANR-17-EGAS-0003."‡Decomposition; Greenhouse gas; Litter; Nitrogen; Nitrous oxide; Soil; UPSaclay‡Dataset‡"ResidueGas_Lashermes_All_Info_And_DataSet.tab

column1: https://doi.org/10.15454/OGJNIC‡https://doi.org/10.15454/OGJNIC‡https://doi.org/10.15454/OGJNIC‡inrae://10.15454/OGJNIC‡‡doi:10.15454/OGJNIC‡2021-02-26T06:25:41Z‡2021-02-25T15:39:05Z‡‡"Dataset for the Global Change Biology paper ""Feasibility of the 4 per 1000 aspirational target for soil carbon. A case study for France""
column2: published as part of the French 4 per mille study."‡Martin
column3: Manuel‡Martin
column4:
Manuel‡"Lien vers les données
Increasing soil organic carbon (SOC) stocks is a promising way to mitigate the increase in atmospheric CO2 concentration. Based on a simple ratio between CO2 anthropogenic emissions and SOC stocks worldwide
column5: it has been suggested that a 0.4% (4 per 1000) yearly increase of SOC stocks could compensate for current anthropogenic CO<sub>2</sub> emissions. Here
column6: we used a reverse RothC modelling approach to estimate the amount of C inputs to soils required to sustain current SOC stocks and to increase them by 4‰ per year over a period  of 30 years. We assessed the feasibility of this aspirational target first by comparing the amount of C inputs required with net primary productivity (NPP) flowing to the soil
column7: and second by considering the SOC saturation concept. Calculations were performed for mainland France
column8: at a 1 km grid cell resolution. This dataset gives the main results supporting these conclusions
column9:
as well as the uncertainty attached to these results."‡Forests and Forest Products; Other; Soils and soil sciences; UPSaclay; carbon; net primary productivity; soil; soil organic carbon‡Dataset‡"balance4p1000meanAll.nc

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/DVBTEQ‡https://doi.org/10.57745/DVBTEQ‡https://doi.org/10.57745/DVBTEQ‡upsaclay://10.57745/DVBTEQ‡en‡doi:10.57745/DVBTEQ‡2024-08-27T10:02:59Z‡2024-08-02T11:45:11Z‡‡Calorimetric analysis of plastics fragments sampled during full scale composting‡DOMENEK-AICHERNIG
column3:
Sandra‡"Lien vers les données
The morphological evolution of the compostable materials during the composting process was assessed by DSC (DSC1
column4: Mettler Toledo
column5: Switzerland) under N2 (flow rate 50 mL/min) using at minimum 1 mg not cleaned solid in 40 µL aluminum pans. Calibration was carried out with Indium and Zinc standards. The samples were scanned with heat-cool-heat program between – 60 and + 180 °C at 10 °C/min heating or cooling rate. Melting enthalpy (Hm) and glass transition temperature (Tg) shown here were analyzed at the first heating scan. If enough material was available
column6: measurements were done in duplicate. Because of the unknown formulation of the samples
column7: the crystallinity degree could not be determined. The Tg of the PHA samples could not be measured
column8:
because the equipment was not sensitive enough."‡UPSaclay; biodegradable packaging; calorimetry; crystallinity; differential scanning calorimetry; glass transition‡Dataset‡"DSC_E10_o_0_R0.txt

column1: https://doi.org/10.15454/DOLRJO‡https://doi.org/10.15454/DOLRJO‡https://doi.org/10.15454/DOLRJO‡inrae://10.15454/DOLRJO‡en‡doi:10.15454/DOLRJO‡2024-05-24T08:48:00Z‡2024-05-24T08:47:14Z‡‡Protective ability of oligosaccharides to preserve lactic acid bacteria (LAB) during freezing
column3: Fernanda‡Cenard
column4:
Stéphanie‡"Lien vers les données
This study concerns the evaluation of the protective ability of oligosaccharides (FOS produced by enzymatic synthesis from sucrose) to preserve three lactic acid bacteria (Lactobacillus delbrueckii ssp bulgaricus CFL1
column5: Lactobacillus plantarum WCFS1
column6: and Carnobacterium maltaromaticum CNCM I-3298) during freezing
column7: freeze-drying at pilot scale
column8: and two weeks of storage of freeze-dried samples at 25°C.  The dataset includes
column9: for each of the three strains: i) the fermentation conditions (temperature
column10: pH
column11: and base consumption); ii)  the stabilization conditions (freezing temperature and containers
column12: freeze-drying recipe and parameters monitored during the cycle); iii) the quantification of the acidifying activity (using the CinAc system
column15: France) and viability (using the plate count method) before and after freezing
column17:

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/9GGBUK‡https://doi.org/10.57745/9GGBUK‡https://doi.org/10.57745/9GGBUK‡upsaclay://10.57745/9GGBUK‡en‡doi:10.57745/9GGBUK‡2024-08-27T09:57:22Z‡2024-08-02T12:21:13Z‡‡Macromolecular weight averages of plastic fragments sampled during the composting experiment‡DOMENEK-AICHERNIG
column3:
Sandra‡"Lien vers les données
The evolution of the macromolecular mass averages and the dispersity of the fragments sampled during the composting process was analyzed with Size-Exclusion Chromatography (SEC). It was performed using a Gilson pump (France) coupled to a Waters autosampler and refractometric index detector (Waters
column4: France). The separation was carried out on a system consisting of a guard column (PLgel 5 µm) and three columns (two columns PLGel 5 µm MIXED-C and one column PLgel 3 µm MIXED E
column5: Agilent Technologies) maintained at 40 °C in a column oven (Waters
column6: France). The flow rate of the CHCl3 was 1 mL/min. The calibration curve was established using three standard kits (EasiVials
column7: 2 mL) containing each four narrow polystyrene standards of molecular weight between 4.69∙103 and 5.68∙106 g/mol (Agilent Technologies). Data treatment was done using Empower 3 software (Waters). Samples were prepared by dissolution of the uncleaned solids in dried CHCl3 (app. 20 mg/mL) at room temperature or after heating at 40 °C without stirring at least 4 h. The supernatant was sampled after sedimentation of the insoluble phase and filtered before analysis with the help of 0.45 µm Teflon syringe filters. Because of the biodegradation of the materials during the process
column8: the less and less sample mass was available. If enough sample available
column9:
the preparations were done several times (at least in duplicate)."‡UPSaclay; biodegradable packaging; molecular weight; packaging material‡Dataset‡"BEC59J216728.arw

column4: Valentin‡Poirier
column5:
Simon‡"Lien vers les données
Data provide detailed information on the diversity of bacterial communities present on 576 samples of raw pork or poultry sausages produced industrially in 2017. Bacterial growth dynamics and diversity were monitored throughout the refrigerated storage period to estimate the impact of packaging atmosphere and the use of potassium lactate as chemical preservative. The data include several types of analysis aiming at providing a comprehensive microbial ecology of spoilage during storage and how the process parameters do influence this phenomenon. The analysis includes: the gas content in packaging
column6: pH
column7: chromametric measurements
column8: plate counts (total mesophilic aerobic flora and lactic acid bacteria)
column10:
meta-metabolomic quantification of volatile organic compounds and bacterial community metagenetic analysis. Bacterial diversity was monitored using two types of amplicon sequencing (16S rRNA and GyrB encoding genes) at different time points for the different conditions (576 samples for gyrB and 436 samples for 16S rDNA). Sequencing data were generated by using Illumina MiSeq. The sequencing data have been deposited in the bioproject PRJNA522361"‡Food Microbiota; Food and food processing; Meat Spoilage; Metabolomic; Metagenetic; Microbial Ecology; Microorganisms; Omics; UPSaclay‡Dataset‡"RDL_TableS01_Sample_Data_V2.tab

The dataset was created as part of a doctoral research project aimed at characterizing and 3D modeling the sedimentary heterogeneities of the Albian clayey-sandy reservoir in the Paris Basin
column6: using an outcrop study of a deltaic reservoir analogue (the Roda Sandstone
column7: southern Pyrenean Basin). This work was supported by the program UPGEO “UPscaling and heat simulations for improving the efficiency of deep GEOthermal energy” funded by the Agence Nationale de la Recherche (ANR-19-CE05-0032-01). This dataset includes the data used and described in the second chapter of the thesis manuscript entitled <i> “Deciphering tidal signature in a fluvial-dominated delta using Digital Outcrop Models (Roda Sandstone
column8: Eocene
column9:
South Pyrenean Basin)”.

This chapter focuses on the sedimentological study of a portion of the Roda Sandstone Formation
column10: referred to as “Y sandbody” in the literature
column11: based on drilling and archive data
column13: and the interpretation of a high-resolution
column14:
georeferenced photogrammetric model. The model covers an area of around 3.3 km² with a resolution of between 0.003 and 0.03 m.

This dataset contains:

  • 20 sedimentological sections from both outcrops and descriptions of 7 cored boreholes
  • A Digital Outcrop Model consisting of 7 georeferenced photogrammetric models

    • The Digital Outcrop Model can be viewed in various 3D visualization

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/LMBUNB‡https://doi.org/10.57745/LMBUNB‡https://doi.org/10.57745/LMBUNB‡inrae://10.57745/LMBUNB‡‡doi:10.57745/LMBUNB‡2023-03-10T09:21:34Z‡2022-09-26T09:55:50Z‡‡Dataset on the Life Cycle Assessment of pizzas produced in different contexts and with real-world variability in consumer practices‡Pénicaud
column3:
Caroline‡"Lien vers les données
Human food consumption is responsible for significant environmental impacts
column4: which in recent years have been the focus of an increasing amount of research. One of the major results of these efforts has been an appreciation for the ways in which impacts can differ among products. To date
column5: though
column6: relatively little is known about possible differences in the environmental performance of a single food product that is made or produced in different contexts. Furthermore
column7: the influence of consumer practices
column8: such as cooking time or cleaning method
column9: has not yet been investigated. This dataset therefore provides information i) to compare the environmental impacts of a single food product—in this case
column10: pizza—that is produced in different contexts (industrial
column12: and assembled at home) and ii) to investigate the influence of real-world consumer practices on these impacts. Two study models were used: a ham-and-cheese pizza and a mixed-cheese pizza. The functional units examined were one pizza and 1 kg of ready-to-eat pizza. The system perimeter extended from the agricultural production of ingredients to the consumption of the pizza at home. All inventory data related to the steps occurring before purchase (including storage at the supermarket) came from databases or the literature
column13: while inventory data related to the steps occurring after the sale were obtained from questionnaires answered by 69 consumers who prepared and consumed the six pizzas (two recipes x three methods of preparation) at home. Background data were selected in the AGRIBALYSE 3.0 and Ecoinvent 3.6 databases. The environmental impacts of the six pizzas were calculated by Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) using the characterization method ""EF 3.0 Method (adapted) V1.00 / EF 3.0 normalization and weighting set” in SimaPro software. To compare the environmental impacts of the six pizzas
column14: 69 LCAs were performed for each; to compensate for missing data from incomplete questionnaires
column15:
we performed random draws from the available data to generate the life cycle inventory for each assessment. The data obtained in this study can be used to make recommendations to consumers regarding more environmentally friendly food choices and practices."‡Life Cycle Assessment; UPSaclay‡Dataset; Text‡"dataset_pizzas_cleaning_method.tab

This dataset comes from the European Union project DROPS (DROught-tolerant yielding PlantS). A panel of 256 maize hybrids was grown with two water regimes (irrigated or rainfed)
column4: in seven fields in 2012 and 2013
column5: respectively
column6: spread along a climatic transect from western to eastern Europe
column7: plus one site in Chile in 2013. This resulted in 29 experiments defined as the combination of one year
column8: one site and one water regime
column9: with two and three repetitions for rainfed and irrigated treatments
column10: respectively. A detailed environmental characterisation was carried out
column11: with hourly records of micrometeorological data and soil water status
column12: and associated with precise measurement of phenology. Grain yield and its components were measured at the end of the experiment. The main purpose of this dataset consists in using the environmental characterisation to quantify the genetic variability of maize grain yield in response to the environmental drivers for genotype-by-environment interaction. For instance

allelic effects at QTLs identified over the field network are consistent within a scenario but largely differ between scenarios."‡Climate; Envirotyping; Genetic variability of grain yield under changing climate; Genotype-by-environment interaction; Multi-environment; Phenotyping; Plant Breeding and Plant Products; UPSaclay; Zea mays‡Dataset‡"0b-MIAPPE-metadata-description.xlsx

The materials batch of the composting experiment containing the compostable plastics was regularly sampled during the process. These sampling was done using the standardized method NF EN 12579:(12579:2013
column4: 2013). For a batch of compost (max 580 m³) it recommends taking 12 elementary samples
column5: each weighing 1 kg
column6: from various locations. The quartering method described in ASTM C702/C702M:2018 (C702/C702M:2018
column7: 2018) was used to reduce the sample size without creating a systematic bias due to product heterogeneity and obtain a representative final sample of around 10 kg per batch and per sampling time. All the compost samples were air-dried. They were then passed through a series of sieves with decreasing mesh sizes to obtain eight fractions ranging from 10 mm to 0.2 mm. Each fraction was weighed
column8: and its water content was determined."‡UPSaclay; biodegradable packaging; composting; granulometry‡Dataset‡"granulometric_fractions.tab"‡etalab 2.0‡https://spdx.org/licenses/etalab-2.0.html‡‡‡‡‡‡

‡Physics-based Simulations of 3D Wave Propagation with Source Variability: HEMEW^S-3D‡Gatti
column6: Filippo‡Lehmann
column7:
Fanny‡"Lien vers les données
The HEMEW^S-3D database contains 30
column8: 000 simulation results of the 3D elastic wave equation. This equation governs the propagation of waves in a 3D propagation medium. Elastic waves originate from a deep source with random location and random orientation. Three types of data are given in this database: a materials dataset
column10: and a velocity dataset.  The major difference between the HEMEW^S-3D database and the previous version HEMEW-3D is the source. It was fixed in the first version while in the current version
column11:
both its position and orientation vary. Each material describes the propagation domain used for one numerical simulation. It is built from non-stationary random fields and corresponds to the velocity of shear waves. All materials are 3D arrays of shape 32 x 32 x 32. A different source is used for each simulation. It is described by its coordinates and the three angles that parametrize its moment tensor. The velocity dataset contains the velocity wavefields simulated at the surface of each propagation domain. Each velocity field has 3 spatial components and 800 time steps (between 0 and 8s). A readme file gives more details on the data and can be found below (search for ""readme"" in the search bar"")"‡Machine Learning; UPSaclay; artificial intelligence; dataset; deep learning; differential equation; digital simulation; kinematic wave; simulation model; three dimensional digital model; time series‡Dataset‡"material100000-101999.npy

column3:

The fine fraction resulting from the sieving (≤12 mm) of the two batches of compost was sent to an independent accredited laboratory (SADEF
column4: France) to analyze the safety and agronomic quality according to the NF U44-051:2006 standard dedicated to organic soil improvers. The safety requirements cover the determination and quantification of inert materials and impurities (XPU 44-164:2004)
column5: pathogen microorganisms as viable helminth eggs (XP X33-017:2004) and Salmonella (NF EN ISO 6579:2002)
column6: HAP trace organic compounds (XP X 33-012:2000) and trace elements (NF EN 13650:2002
column7: NF EN ISO 17294-2:2016). The agronomic assessment of the two composts covers the determination of total solid
column8: organic matter
column10: total nitrogen C/N ratio
column17: total sulphur content according to standardized methods (NF EN 13040:2007
column21: NF EN ISO 17294-2:2016). A phytotoxicity test was carried out on both compost batches (NF U44-167:2016) to assess the risk of inhibiting the emergence and growth of two higher plants (barley
column25: type Alénois). The acute toxicity test on earthworms (Eisenia fetida) was carried out in accordance with standard NF ISO 11268-1:2012. The immobilization test of microcrustaceans was carried out on Daphnia magma (ISO 6341:2012 using an eluate of the two composts obtained with a liquid/solid mass ratio of 10 (calculated as dry matter equivalent) as described in the leaching protocol of standard EN 12457-2:2002."‡UPSaclay; compost; ecotoxicity‡Dataset‡"compost_quality.xlsx"‡etalab 2.0‡https://spdx.org/licenses/etalab-2.0.html‡‡‡‡‡‡

GWAS results used for the analysis of 4 multi-environment trials experiments data through metaGE meta-analysis:

- The Arabidopsis dataset of Frachon et al. 2017
column4: where GWAS analyses were performed on the local mapping population TOU-A
column5: with a panel of 195 whole-genome sequenced accessions evaluated in six controlled micro-habitats (combinations of three soils × presence/absence of inter-specific competition
column6: noted A to F). Each accession was genotyped at 981
column7:
278 SNPs (after quality control and a MAF threshold of 0.07) and phenotyped for bolting time.

- The maize Amaizing-DROPS dataset of Millet et al. 2016
column8: where GWAS analyses were performed on a panel of 244 maize dent lines evaluated as hybrids with a common parental line in 22 environments (combinations of location × year x treatment). Each line was genotyped at 602
column9: 356 SNPs (after quality control) and phenotyped for grain yield in the 22 environments. In addition
column10:
environmental variables were measured in each environment.

- The multi-parental population maize EU-NAM Flint dataset of Bauer et al. 2013 and Lehermeier et al. 2014
column11: which includes 11 biparental populations obtained from crosses between UH007 and 11 peripheral parents representative of the Northern Europe maize diversity. In each population
column12: double haploid (DH) lines were produced and genotyped at 5
column13: 263 SNPs (after quality control). All populations were evaluated for biomass dry matter yield (DMY) in 4 locations. Three populations with less than 30 progenies were removed from the present analysis. GWAS analyses were performed on each combination of cross and location
column14:
resulting in 32 individual analysis.

- The Wheat dataset of Robert et al. 2020
column15: where GWAS analyses were performed on a panel of 210 wheat lines phenotyped for grain yield in 16 environments (combinations of location x year x treatment). Lines were genotyped at 108

column3:
Caroline‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset presents Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) of the production of FOS and GOS by enzymatic synthesis from glucose (to get FOS) or lactose (to get GOS) and hydrolytic production from extraction of yacon potato (to get FOS) or chickpea (to get GOS). A cradle-to-gate approach was considered in the two scenarios under assessment (the phases of use and/or final disposal of FOS/GOS were not considered). The functional unit was defined as 100 g of FOS/GOS produced. LCAs were performed using data collected at the laboratory scale
column4: supplemented with data from Ecoinvent database. SimaPro was used for the LCA modeling with the midpoint impact EF2.0 characterization method. For each scenario (FOS produced by synthesis
column5: FOS produced by hydrolysis
column6: GOS produced by synthesis
column7: GOS produced by hydrolysis)

Mehdi‡"Lien vers les données
We present here a new gridded dataset of per capita disposable income consistent with the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) over the 21st century in Europe. Decadal income maps were created using a top-down iterative approach that ensures quantitative consistency with other SSP socio-economic datasets (GDP
column4: demographics projections

column6:
etc.). This dataset is structured into 39 sub-folders (one for each European country + the whole studied area). column3:
Celine‡"Lien vers les données
This dataset presents two sets of raw near infrared spectra of different organic samples acquired on three different spectrometers in three labs: NIRFlex N-500 FT-NIR (BUCHI
column4: France
column5: serial number 1000001305) at LBE in Narbonne
column6: NIR-System5000 (FOSS
column7: Denmark
column8: serial number 94630632) at SELMET in Montpellier and ANTARIS II FT-NIR (ThermoFisher Scientific
column10: serial number AHY0800337) at IJPB in Versailles.  A first set of spectra acquired in the same conditions on the three devices was used to create spectra transfer models between the three spectrometers (two by two). Transfer models were created thanks to the Piecewise Direct Standardisation (PDS) function. The resulting spectra transfer model is a transform matrix
column11: allowing to transform spectra by matrix multiplication. Appendix 2 is an example of transform matrix allowing to transform FOSS spectra into BUCHI spectra. The other five transfer models were established in a same way and transform matrices were used alike. A second set of independent spectra was also acquired on the three devices to validate transfer models between spectrometers. Spectra analyses and models were created on ChemFlow (https://vm-chemflow-francegrille.eu/)
column12: a free online chemometric software that includes all the necessary functions.   This work was supported by the Biomass For the Future project from the French National Research Agency (ANR

column1: https://doi.org/10.57745/VUCKWN‡https://doi.org/10.57745/VUCKWN‡https://doi.org/10.57745/VUCKWN‡espace-generique://10.57745/VUCKWN‡fr‡doi:10.57745/VUCKWN‡2024-09-11T12:08:45Z‡2024-08-01T13:15:11Z‡‡Données de thèse - Facteurs de contrôle sur le développement d’une plateforme carbonatée et son évolution diagénétique: impact sur les porosités matricielle et karstique. Cas du Jurassique moyen et de l’Oxfordien du Bassin Nord-Aquitain‡Deloume-Carpentras
column3:
Quentin‡"Lien vers les données
Le jeu de données a été réalisé dans le cadre du projet de thèse
column4: qui vise à mieux appréhender les caractéristiques géologiques des calcaires du Jurassique moyen et de l’Oxfordien du Bassin Nord-Aquitain. Pour répondre aux problématiques scientifiques spécifiques
column5: de nombreuses données sédimentologiques
column6: diagénétiques
column7: géochimiques et géochronologiques ont été acquises. Les données de la partie (« sédimentologie ») de la thèse permettent de quantifier l’influence couplée de l’accommodation et des fluctuations climatiques à long terme sur la production carbonatée et les types de producteurs carbonatés
column8: que ce soit à l’échelle du Bassin Aquitain mais également sur l’ouest de la France. Les données sont issues d’affleurements et de forages. Les taux d’accommodation (m/Ma) et taux d’accommodation affranchie de l’isostasie (m/Ma) ont été calculés pour 5 périodes géologiques dans 37 localités dans le Bassin Nord-Aquitain et dans 70 localités dans l’ouest du Bassin de Paris (« Annexe1_chapitres 4 et 5_accommodation »). Les taux d’accumulation de carbonates (m/Ma) sont calculés dans les mêmes localités et pour les mêmes périodes géologiques (« Annexe2_chapitres 4 et 5_accumulation de carbonates »). Un type de producteur carbonaté dominant est associé pour ces 5 intervalles stratigraphiques sur chacune des localités du Bassin Nord-Aquitain et de l’ouest du Bassin de Paris (« Annexe3_chapitres 4 et 5_producteurs carbonatés »). Les données acquises dans la partie (« diagenèse des carbonates ») de la thèse permettent de reconstruire l’histoire diagénétique et paléohydrologique de la bordure nord-est du Bassin Aquitain et de proposer une méthodologie pour dater les processus diagénétiques (néomorphisme
column9: dolomitisation et dissolution/karstification) et in fine pour comprendre leur influence sur la distribution des propriétés réservoirs. Ces données ont été acquises sur des ciments carbonatés (calcites et dolomites). Les compositions en éléments majeurs (Ca
column10: Mg
column13: Al et Sr) ont été mesurées à l'aide de la microsonde électronique SX-Five (« Annexe4_chapitre6_données microsonde électronique »). Les données isotopiques en δ13C et δ18O ont été acquises par deux méthodes : (1) par spectrométrie de masse à rapports isotopiques (« isotope-ratio mass spectrometry »
column14: IRMS) et (2) par spectrométrie de masse des ions secondaires (« secondary ion mass spectrometry »
column15: SIMS). Des mesures en δ18O et δ13C ont également été effectuées à partir de l'analyse des « clumped isotopes » (IRMS Δ47). Toutes les données des isotopes stables de l’oxygène et du carbone sont rassemblées dans le fichier « Annexe5_chapitre 6_données IRMS et SIMS ». Les compositions en éléments majeurs
column16: mineurs et traces et la composition en éléments des terres rares ont été mesurées à l'aide d’un spectromètre de masse à plasma à couplage inductif haute résolution Element XR de Thermo ScientificTM couplé à un système d’ablation laser (LA-HR-ICP-MS) et synthétisées dans le fichier « Annexe 6_chapitre 6_majeurs
column18: traces et ETR ». Les données utilisées pour caractériser l’évolution de l’histoire d’enfouissement et d’exhumation de la bordure nord-est du Bassin Aquitain
column19: modélisée à partir du logiciel Petromod 1D
column20: ont été regroupées dans le fichier « Annexe7_chapitre 6_Petromod1D ». Le traitement des données issues de la méthode de datation U-Pb sur calcites à partir d’un LA-HR-ICP-MS est effectué à partir du fichier « Annexe8_chapitres 6 et 7_traitement données U-Pb ». Enfin
column21: la description détaillée de soixante-sept affleurements (« Annexe9_coupes sédimentaires ») permet de déterminer la lithologie
column24: les structures sédimentaires et biogéniques.   The dataset was produced as part of a thesis project in order to better understanding the geological characteristics of the Middle Jurassic and Oxfordian limestones of the northern Aquitaine Basin. Consequently
column28: geochemical and geochronological data was acquired. The data in the ""sedimentology” part of the thesis enable us to quantify the coupled influence of accommodation and long-term eccentricity variations on carbonate accumulation rates for numerous carbonate factories during Middle Jurassic to Oxfordian
column29: both at the scale of the Aquitaine Basin and in western France. Data were obtained from outcrops and boreholes. Accommodation rates (m/Ma) and non-isostatic accommodation rates (m/Ma) were calculated for 5 geological periods in 37 localities in the northern Aquitain Basin and in 70 localities in the western Paris Basin (""Annexe1_chapitres 4 et 5_accommodation""). The carbonate accumulation rates (m/Ma) are calculated in the same localities and for the same geological periods (""Annexe2_chapitres 4 et 5_accumulation de carbonates""). A dominant type of carbonate producer is associated with each of the localities in the northern Aquitaine Basin and western Paris Basin for these 5 stratigraphic intervals (""Annexe3_chapitres 4 et 5_producteurs carbonatés""). The data acquired in the ""carbonate diagenesis"" part of the thesis enable us to reconstruct the diagenetic and paleohydrological history of the northeastern Aquitaine Basin and to propose a newly innovative methodology for dating diagenetic processes (neomorphism
column30: dolomitization and dissolution/karstification) and ultimately for understanding their influence on the distribution of reservoir properties. These data were acquired on carbonate cements (calcites and dolomites). The major element compositions (Ca
column34: Al and Sr) were measured using the SX-Five electron microprobe (""Annexe4_chapitre6_données microsonde électronique""). Isotopic data in δ13C and δ18O were acquired by two methods: (1) isotope-ratio mass spectrometry (IRMS) and (2) secondary ion mass spectrometry (SIMS). Measurements in δ18O and δ13C were also made using clumped isotope analysis (IRMS Δ47). The oxygen and carbon isotopic data are compiled in the file ""Annexe5_chapitre 6_données IRMS et SIMS"". Major
column36: trace and Rare Earth Element compositions were measured using a laser ablation coupled to a sector field inductively plasma mass spectrometer from Thermo Scientific (LA-HR-ICP-MS) and summarized in the file ""Annexe 6_chapitre 6_majeurs
column38: traces et ETR"". The data used to characterize the evolution of the burial and exhumation history of the northeastern Aquitaine Basin
column40: have been compiled in the file ""Annexe7_chapitre 6_Petromod1D"". Data from the LA-HR-ICP-MS method of U-Pb dating on calcites are processed in the file ""Annexe8_chapitre 6 et 7_traitement données U-Pb”. Finally
column41: the detailed description of sixty-seven outcrop sections (« Annexe9_coupes sédimentaires ») provides information on lithology
